comments.author: @NICEGLOBE05
comments.text: People often mistake Dutch directness for being 'shameless' or rude, but it’s actually the highest form of honesty. In the Netherlands, they don't waste time with fake politeness or sugarcoating. They tell you the truth to your face because they respect your time. Once you get used to it, it’s incredibly refreshing! Brilliant documentary!

In the video, we wanted to show how the Netherlands has a very open and direct way of looking at life, especially compared with many other countries. Every culture has its own way of understanding modesty, beauty, and freedom.

comments.author: @powskier
comments.text: "Step back 400 years to the Dutch golden age.". Yo mean when the Dutch ruled the slave trade?

replies.author: @powskier
replies.text: ​@AmazingGlobalDiscoveries Then you had mass murdering Belgium right next door who committed genocide and horrific crimes in the Congo

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: You’re absolutely right to bring that up. The Dutch Golden Age also had a very dark side, including colonial exploitation and involvement in the slave trade. It’s important not to romanticize that history. In the video, the point was to show how influential that period was, but your comment is a good reminder that influence came with painful consequences for many people. Thank you for adding this perspective.

comments.author: @powskier
comments.text: All this video of the Dutch and not even one of them was wearing clogs.  Disappointed.

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: Haha, fair point! The clogs are such an iconic image of the Netherlands, but in daily life you don’t see them very often anymore. Maybe we should have included at least one pair for tradition’s sake. Thanks for watching and pointing that out, my friend!

comments.author: @johannesversendaal4554
comments.text: I was born in Rotterdam. My parents immigrated to the US in 1961 I was young but smarter with kids my age, since the education system there is way better. I have gone back a few times. One time in the seventies my Aunt's boyfriend owned several bars and a block of red light district girls. I remember he had a police scanner for a heads up if he was going to be raided, anyway free sex and booze the whole time I was there I was 22 and loving it. One day I went to Amsterdam and of course the red light district. Lots of beautiful women it was hard to choose, as I was turned on by one lady the negotiation was on, we agreed on 30 minutes for 20 american dollars, husband and kids where in the back eating dinner. I will never forget those thirty minutes. The museums are something to see. Those dutch paintings you see are huge like 12 feet wide by 10 feet tall I was amazed. The next few times I went I had to behave because I was married. Also the first time I went back my parents celebrated their 25 wedding anniversary 350+ people attended and most where related. If you go to Europe you have got to go to The Netherlands (Holland) and enjoy.

replies.author: @AV8RRRR
replies.text: Husband & Kids? so that means you are a woman & you secretly had sex with another woman! wow that is an EXCITING mission you pulled off! my papa's name is Johannes our family migrated to the USA in the 50's from the Netherlands <3 I need to go back there, last time I was there I was 9 years old.

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text:
Thank you for sharing such a personal memory. Rotterdam, Amsterdam, family gatherings, museums, and the old Dutch atmosphere clearly left a deep mark on your life. It is amazing how one country can hold so many different sides, from history and art to nightlife and family traditions.
I especially liked what you said about the museums and those huge Dutch paintings. That part is easy to forget when people only talk about Amsterdam’s famous districts. The Netherlands really is a place full of contrasts.

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries

The Netherlands can feel very surprising to people from more traditional places, but that contrast is exactly what makes it so interesting to discuss. Thank you for sharing your honest perspective, my friend.

comments.author: @hojo1948
comments.text: Lived a few months in Holland as a young man in the 1970's.  Did see "the red light district" in Amsterdam where scantily clad ladies displayed their wares to the passerbys - fishing for business for those willing to come in and pay and YES "red lighting" was the norm.  Also saw topless sunbathing at the public swimming pools.  Overaall a very clean, vibrant place - well at least in Amsterdam and Delft, nice people.  Nice disco in Delft - one young girl there most nights simply wore out the dance floor to ABBA'a "Dancing Queen" - and she was! Leaving teh club one night ran into "Kung Fu Fighting" going on in the street outside with a small group of teens at least going thru tyhe motions - of the irony as they had played that song not long before we left the disco.  Great times....

replies.author: @Mundivo
replies.text: @UnseenWorldDocs_Us Do you prefer life in the 1970s or modern times?

replies.author: @UnseenWorldDocs_Us
replies.text: @Mundivo Vibrant 1970s freedom vs cold tech control? 📜

replies.author: @Mundivo
replies.text: What do you see as the biggest change in the Netherlands compared to the 1970s?

replies.author: @UnseenWorldDocs_Us
replies.text: Fascinating 1970s vibe! Pure respect for living that vibrant, open Dutch history!

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text:
What a wonderful memory to share. The Netherlands in the 1970s must have had such a unique energy, especially in Amsterdam and Delft. I love how you described the red light district, the topless sunbathing, the clean streets, the disco nights, and even that funny “Kung Fu Fighting” moment outside the club. It sounds like a time full of freedom, music, and unforgettable little scenes from everyday life.
Thank you for bringing your personal experience into the discussion. Stories like yours make the history and culture feel much more alive.

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: Yes, my friend, BeNeLux is the name formed from Belgium, the Netherlands, and Luxembourg. These three countries are often grouped together because of their close geography, history, and cooperation. Some people also connect this region with the “Low Countries.”

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries

You’re right, the Netherlands has had a major influence in the global energy and refinery industry, especially through its strong trading and port network. We couldn’t cover every detail in one video, but this is definitely a topic worth exploring more deeply. Thank you for adding this extra perspective.
